content.text: React as a UI Library

React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces. It is not a full framework — it has one job: describing and rendering UI as a tree of components. Everything else (routing, data fetching, form handling) is handled by separate libraries that you pick as needed. This narrow focus is a deliberate choice that keeps React small, predictable, and composable.

attrs.alt: React component tree showing App root with Header, Main, Footer children; Main has ProductList and Cart; ProductList has two ProductCard leaves. Right panel shows four core concepts: components are functions, props are read-only inputs, state is private memory, declarative rendering via UI=f(state,props)
attrs.title: React component model

content.text: The Component Model

The core abstraction in React is the component. A component is a function that accepts data (props) and returns a description of what should appear on screen (JSX). React calls your functions, collects the returned JSX, and maps it to real DOM nodes.
Two rules make components predictable:
Component names start with a capital letter.
React uses this to distinguish components (
<Button />
) from HTML elements (
<button />
Components must be pure.
Given the same props and state, a component must always return the same JSX. Side effects (API calls, DOM reads) belong in effects, not in the render body.
JSX: Describing the UI
JSX is a syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like markup inside JavaScript. It compiles to regular JavaScript function calls — every JSX element becomes a call to
React.createElement
(or the newer JSX transform equivalent).
JSX embeds JavaScript expressions using curly braces. Any valid expression can appear inside
: variables, function calls, ternary operators, array maps.
JSX vs HTML differences
HTML
JSX equivalent
Reason
class="btn"
className="btn"
class is a reserved word in JS
for="email"
htmlFor="email"
for is a reserved word in JS
onclick="fn()"
onClick={fn}
camelCase events; pass function reference not string
JSX requires self-closing tags
Multiple root elements allowed
Single root required (or
fragment)
Return value must be one expression
Props: Data Flowing Downward

Props (short for properties) are the inputs a parent passes to a child component. They are read-only inside the component — a component never modifies its own props. This enforces a clear data ownership model: the parent decides what the child renders.
Props can be any JavaScript value: strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, objects, functions, or even other React elements (
children
State: Private Component Memory

State is data owned and managed by a single component. When state changes, React re-renders the component (and its subtree) to reflect the new value. Unlike props, state is private and mutable — but only via the setter function returned by
useState
Three things happen when you call a setter:
React schedules a re-render of the component.
The component function runs again with the new state value.
React updates only the DOM nodes that changed (not the whole page).
State is a snapshot

Each render has its own snapshot of state. Closures inside a render capture the state value at the time they were created — not the latest value. This is why event handlers "see" the state from the render they were defined in.
Declarative vs Imperative UI

Before React, building UIs meant imperative DOM manipulation: finding elements, updating their text, toggling classes, and managing a mental model of what the DOM currently looks like. React replaces this with a declarative model: you describe what the UI should look like for a given state, and React figures out how to make the DOM match.
The declarative approach scales better: as your UI grows in complexity, you continue describing each state of the UI rather than managing every possible transition between states.
One-Way Data Flow

In React, data flows in one direction: from parent to child via props. A child component cannot modify its parent's state directly. Instead, it calls a callback function passed as a prop, which the parent uses to update its own state.
One-way data flow makes apps easier to debug: if something renders incorrectly, you know the problem is in the component that owns the state (or in how the state is being passed down).
Composition: Building Complex UIs from Simple Parts

React's model is composable by design. You build complex UIs by combining simple components — the same way you compose functions in JavaScript. React does not use inheritance for this; composition (nesting components) is the correct pattern.
Why composition beats inheritance
Explicit data flow.
Inherited methods and state are implicit — props are not. You can trace exactly what data a component uses.
No coupling to class hierarchy.
You can replace or swap a composed child without touching the parent class.
Easier to test.
Each component can be tested in isolation with any props you choose.
The Virtual DOM

When a component's state or props change, React re-renders that component — meaning it calls the function again and produces a new JSX tree. Rather than updating the real DOM immediately, React compares the new tree against the previous one (a process called reconciliation or diffing) and applies only the minimal set of DOM operations needed.

The Virtual DOM is React's in-memory representation of the UI. You do not interact with it directly — it is an implementation detail. What matters to you as a developer is the guarantee: describe what the UI should look like, and React keeps the DOM in sync efficiently.
Step
What happens
1. Trigger
State/prop change causes React to schedule a re-render
2. Render
React calls your component functions and builds a new virtual tree
3. Diff
New virtual tree is compared against the previous one (reconciliation)
4. Commit
React applies only the changed DOM operations (minimal updates)
Thinking in Components
A useful exercise for any new UI is to break the mockup into a component hierarchy before writing code. Ask three questions for each region:
Does this region have its own data?
If it manages independent state (a toggle, a counter, a form), it is a component.
Is it reused in multiple places?
Reuse is not required for components, but it is a strong signal.
Can it be described in one sentence?
A component that does too many things should be split.

The React team's recommended approach (from their docs) is: start with a static version (no state), identify the minimal representation of state needed, figure out where state should live (the highest component that needs it), and add inverse data flow (callbacks). This top-down approach scales from a single button to enterprise applications.
Single responsibility per component
Each component should do one thing well. A
ProductCard
renders a single product. A
ProductList
arranges a collection of them. A
FilterBar
handles filtering logic. When a component grows to 200+ lines or handles unrelated concerns, it is time to extract.
Rendering and the Component Lifecycle
In function components (the modern React style), the lifecycle maps to three moments:
Moment
What it means
Hook / mechanism
Mount
Component appears in the tree for the first time
Update
State or props change — component re-renders
useEffect(() => { ... }, [dep])
Unmount
Component is removed from the tree
Cleanup function returned from useEffect

Effects and lifecycle details are covered in depth in the Hooks section of this course. At this stage, the key insight is that a component has a clear lifetime: it mounts when added to the tree, updates when its data changes, and unmounts when removed.
React Is Just a Library
React is intentionally minimal. It handles exactly one concern: rendering UI from component trees. This means:
You choose your own router (React Router, TanStack Router, Next.js router).
You choose your own state management (useState, Zustand, Redux, Jotai).
You choose your own data fetching (fetch, TanStack Query, SWR, Apollo).
You choose your own styling (CSS modules, Tailwind, styled-components, Emotion).

This means a React project's stack is not fully determined by React itself — you assemble the pieces. This flexibility is both a strength (you pick the right tool for your needs) and a responsibility (you need to make those choices). Frameworks like Next.js and Remix make these decisions for you while staying built on React.
Common Beginner Mistakes
Understanding the component model helps avoid a class of errors that trip up developers new to React.
Mutating state directly
State must be replaced, not mutated. React tracks changes by comparing the old and new state reference. If you mutate the object in place, React cannot detect the change and will not re-render.
Putting derived values in state
If a value can be computed from existing state or props, compute it during render — do not store it in state. Duplicate state creates synchronisation bugs.
Missing the key prop in lists
When you render a list of components, React needs a stable
key
prop to identify each element across renders. Without a key, React falls back to position-based identity, which breaks when the list is reordered or items are added or removed.
Defining components inside other components

Defining a component inside another component's function body causes React to treat it as a new component type on every render, which destroys and recreates the DOM node — losing focus, scroll position, and internal state.
When to Split a Component
There is no fixed rule for when to extract a component, but several signals reliably indicate it is time:
Signal
Action
JSX block appears more than once
Extract it into a component (even if only used twice)
Function body exceeds ~100 lines
Look for a cohesive sub-section to extract
Comment needed to explain a JSX block
The comment is the component's name — extract it
Shared state between two sibling sections
Lift state to a parent component
Section that could be tested in isolation
Extract it — components are the unit of testing in React
Summary
React is a UI library, not a framework — it renders component trees and nothing else.
Components are pure functions:
(props, state) => JSX
. Same inputs, same output.
Props flow down from parent to child; events flow up via callbacks.
State is private per-component memory; changing it triggers a re-render.
The declarative model (describe the target state, let React diff and update) replaces imperative DOM manipulation.
Composition — nesting components — is the primary pattern for building complex UIs.
